t: For moderate-severe OSA; AHI significantly reduced, mortality reduced.
BiPAP — in refractory cases or CPAP non-response.
Weight loss — mild OSA; bariatric surgery in severe obesity.
Mandibular advancement device (MAD) — mild-moderate OSA, CPAP non-responsive.
Surgery (UPPP, hypoglossal nerve stimulation — Inspire) — in selected cases.
New surgical option; in selected cases.
Without treatment — stroke, atrial fibrillation, diabetes, increased mortality.
Myth 1: "OSA only occurs in obese people"
Possible in normal-weight patients as well (anatomical factors).
Myth 2: "Snoring is the main sign of OSA, nothing more is needed"
snoring alone not proof; AHI measurement required; untreated OSA high mortality and morbidity.
Myth 3: "CPAP only reduces symptoms, does not affect mortality"
Myth 4: "Central sleep apnea and OSA are treated the same"
Different intervention (ASV — Adaptive Servo-Ventilation for central apnea); differentiation is critical.
Kapur V.K. et al. AASM Clinical Practice Guideline for Diagnostic Testing for Adult OSA. J Clin Sleep Med 2017;13(3):479–504.
Marin J.M. et al. Long-term cardiovascular outcomes in men with OSA-hypopnoea with or without treatment with CPAP. Lancet 2005;365(9464):1046–1053.
